frameworks.name: Pavlovian-instrumental arbitration
frameworks.one_line: Brain arbitrates between Pavlovian and instrumental controllers by predictive accuracy

frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: Stimulus-only vs stimulus-action value learners compete for control

frameworks.name: controllability inference
frameworks.one_line: Inferred reward controllability gates which predictor wins

core_question: Does the balance between Pavlovian and instrumental control depend on inferred reward controllability?
key_assumptions: Pavlovian predictor learns values from stimuli only
Instrumental predictor learns values from stimuli and actions
Brain arbitrates by choosing the better reward predictor
Pavlovian bias is a state, not a fixed individual trait
manipulations: Low vs high controllability contexts over rewards
Cue valence crossed with required approach/avoid action
key_findings: Pavlovian approach bias was stronger under low control than high control
Within-individual variation of Pavlovian bias across contexts
Bias modulation consistent with arbitration by predictor accuracy
interpretations: Pavlovian bias reflects adaptive arbitration, not a fixed trait
When actions matter little, the simpler Pavlovian predictor is favored
